title: The five stages of a USMCA shakeup
gist: Atlantic Council's Madeline Chalecki forecasts a multi-year, five-stage unraveling of USMCA after the US signals on July 1, 2026 that it will not renew the pact — moving from initial business-as-usual through tariff escalation (a potential $70 billion hit via Section 301), fragmentation into bilateral deals, and economic drag on the tariff-exposed auto sector, with China positioned as the chief beneficiary of a divided North America.

insights.claim: On July 1, 2026 the United States is set to formally announce it will not renew the USMCA, beginning a potentially decade-long negotiation process, though the announcement alone will not immediately terminate the agreement.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: Marks the start of a prolonged period of North American trade uncertainty rather than a clean break — investors should model this as a years-long overhang, not a single-day shock.
insights.locator: On July 1, North America may enter a new era

insights.claim: In the initial phase, Canada and Mexico are likely to continue business as usual, absorbing tariff threats and pursuing bilateral talks (Mexico) or calls for quick renewal (Canada) rather than escalating, because their economies remain deeply integrated with and dependent on the US.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: Suggests near-term market complacency is rational in the first stage, but the piece argues this calm is temporary as negotiation leverage erodes over months.
insights.locator: Despite unprecedented tariffs and US President Donald Trump’s insistence

insights.claim: If talks stall, Washington could expand Section 232 tariffs, launch new Section 232 investigations, tighten USMCA eligibility rules, or apply Section 301 tariffs regardless of USMCA status; a Section 301 move alone, at the 25% rate used under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act before the USMCA exemption, could add roughly $70 billion in tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: A specific, sizeable tariff tail-risk figure ($70B) to price into North American supply-chain and import-exposed equity exposure if negotiations sour.
insights.locator: Tariffs would remain on the table

insights.claim: Existing Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs have already strained US-Canada-Mexico relations, making their removal a top priority for both Canada and Mexico even before any further escalation.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: Identifies steel/aluminum tariff removal as the near-term bellwether issue to watch for signs of whether the relationship is stabilizing or deteriorating.

insights.claim: As talks drag on, the trilateral USMCA framework could dissolve into bilateral deals, since Trump prefers bilateral agreements and could strike one with Mexico, which has weathered recent trade tensions with Washington better than Canada has.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: A US-Mexico bilateral deal without Canada would reshape auto and manufacturing supply chains and could be a tradeable divergence between the two economies.
insights.locator: As negotiations drag on, new agreements could play a bigger role

insights.claim: China would be one of the biggest winners of a fragmented North America: Canada has already deepened economic ties with China (negotiating expanded exports and eased tariffs), and Mexico — having reduced Chinese-import dependence under US pressure — would have greater reason to turn back to China for inputs, investment, and low-cost goods absent renewal incentives.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: Flags China as the structural beneficiary of USMCA fragmentation — a reason to watch Canada-China and Mexico-China trade data as leading indicators of North American disintegration.
insights.locator: China would be one of the biggest winners

insights.claim: The North American auto sector — the largest component of total regional trade — has already seen tariffs rise nearly 625% due to Section 232, producing a 10% drop in imports and a 19% drop in exports in the year after 'Liberation Day' compared with the prior year, despite the sector's heavy reliance on USMCA preferences.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence the auto sector is already absorbing severe tariff damage; it is the highest-beta sector to monitor if USMCA renewal talks deteriorate further.
insights.locator: For example, the auto sector, the largest component

insights.claim: Because supply chains are built with roughly thirty-year visibility rather than five, prolonged USMCA negotiation uncertainty — even absent new tariff hikes — is enough on its own to deter investment and slow growth across the region.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: Explains why capex and reshoring decisions could stall well before any tariff actually lands — a signal to watch corporate capex guidance for North American manufacturing.
insights.quote: Supply chains are built with thirty-year visibility, not five
insights.locator: Even if additional tariff hikes don’t materialize

insights.claim: Chalecki frames the USMCA shakeup as mirroring the five stages of grief after a breakup: denial (belief the relationship continues unchanged), anger (tariffs and retaliation), bargaining (side deals and diversification), and eventually acceptance of a new — likely harder — reality.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: A useful mental model for sequencing trade-headline risk over the coming years — investors can map future news (retaliatory tariffs, bilateral talks, new pacts) onto specific stages of the cycle.
insights.quote: First comes denial, as policymakers convince themselves that the relationship can continue as before. Then comes anger, expressed through tariffs and retaliation.
insights.locator: If these stages have sounded familiar

insights.claim: The eventual outcome is uncertain: North America could emerge with a new trilateral or bilateral framework better suited to today's challenges (China competition, AI, labor and environment issues, supply-chain shocks), or years of negotiation could be wasted preserving an agreement that no longer fits reality.
insights.speaker: Madeline Chalecki
insights.so_what: Frames the terminal risk as bimodal — either a modernized trade framework (bullish for regional integration) or years of wasted, growth-sapping uncertainty (bearish) — with no clear base case yet.
insights.locator: From the chaos, a new trilateral agreement or new bilateral deals may rise
